title: Treacle tart
subtitle: Open shortcrust tart with a golden syrup and breadcrumb filling
excerpt: A blind-baked shortcrust pastry case filled with a mixture of golden syrup, fresh breadcrumbs, lemon juice, and double cream. The breadcrumbs absorb the syrup and set the filling during baking so it cuts cleanly. Served warm with custard or clotted cream. One of the few British puddings named for an ingredient it does not contain: golden syrup, not treacle.
sourceNotes: Treacle tart appears in British cookbooks from the late Victorian period, after Abram Lyle and Sons introduced golden syrup commercially in 1883. Despite its name, the filling has always been made with golden syrup (a refined cane sugar syrup), not black treacle, which would be far too bitter. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845, Project Gutenberg), gives an early version of a syrup tart with breadcrumbs, and the combination became standard in British domestic baking through the early 20th century. The recipe here uses fresh breadcrumbs, which give a softer set than dried, and a small amount of cream to enrich the filling.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pastry case can be blind-baked a day ahead. Fill and bake on the day. The tart keeps at room temperature for 2 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 160°C. The filling should be set and beginning to pull away from the edges when done. A slight wobble in the centre is fine; it firms as it cools.

glossaryTerms.definition: Golden syrup is an inverted sugar syrup with a distinctive buttery, slightly caramelised flavour. In treacle tart it functions as both flavour and filling: heated in the oven, it flows into the bread crumbs and sets as it cools. The breadcrumbs absorb the syrup and prevent the filling from being liquid when cut. Lemon juice is added to cut through the sweetness. Too few breadcrumbs and the filling does not set; too many and the texture becomes stodgy rather than soft.

content.text: The filling of treacle tart is built on

. Warm the syrup slightly before mixing so it flows into the crumbs evenly. The ratio of crumbs to syrup determines the set: too few crumbs and it will not cut cleanly, too many and it becomes heavy. The lemon juice is not optional; without it the filling is cloying.

attrs.prepNote: yolk only, mixed with 2 tbsp cold water

content.text: Rub the cold butter into the flour and icing sugar until the mixture resembles fine breadcrumbs. Add the egg yolk and water and bring together into a smooth pastry. Flatten into a disc, wrap, and refrigerate for 30 minutes. Roll out to 3 mm thickness and line a 23 cm loose-bottomed tart tin. Trim the edges and refrigerate for 15 minutes. Heat the oven to 180°C conventional (160°C fan). Line the pastry case with baking paper, fill with baking beans or rice, and blind-bake for 15 minutes. Remove the paper and beans and bake for a further 5 minutes until pale gold. Leave to cool slightly.

Warm the golden syrup in a saucepan over low heat until it flows easily. Stir in the fresh breadcrumbs, lemon juice, lemon zest, and double cream. Pour the filling into the pastry case. Bake for 20 to 25 minutes until the filling is set at the edges but has a slight wobble in the centre. Leave to cool in the tin for 15 minutes before removing. Serve warm with custard or clotted cream.
